# Exim C → Rust Migration: Benchmark Report

**Report Generated:** __REPORT_DATE__

**Exim Version:** 4.99-dev (C) / 4.99-dev (Rust)

**Report Tool:** Generated by `bench/run_benchmarks.sh`

---

## Executive Summary

This report presents a quantitative performance comparison between the original C implementation and the new Rust implementation of the Exim Mail Transfer Agent (version 4.99-dev). Four key performance metrics are measured and evaluated against predefined acceptance thresholds as specified in the migration acceptance criteria.

| Gate | Metric | Verdict |
|------|--------|---------|
| 1 | SMTP Transaction Throughput | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_VERDICT__ |
| 2 | Fork-per-Connection Latency | __FORK_LATENCY_VERDICT__ |
| 3 | Peak RSS Memory Usage | __PEAK_RSS_VERDICT__ |
| 4 | Config Parse Time | __CONFIG_PARSE_VERDICT__ |

**Overall Migration Readiness:** __OVERALL_VERDICT__

---

## Methodology

Each benchmark follows a rigorous methodology designed for statistical significance and reproducibility. All tests are automated via the `bench/run_benchmarks.sh` script.

### General Principles

- All benchmarks run sequentially on an otherwise-idle system to minimize interference.
- Each benchmark is preceded by a warm-up phase to prime filesystem caches and eliminate cold-start variance.
- Both the C and Rust binaries are tested under identical configurations using the same `configure.default` file.
- Results are captured in structured JSON and CSV formats for post-processing.

### 2.1 SMTP Transaction Throughput

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Tool** | `swaks` (Swiss Army Knife for SMTP) |
| **Message Count** | 10,000 messages per run |
| **Delivery Method** | Localhost (127.0.0.1) loopback delivery |
| **Configuration** | Default `src/src/configure.default` with local delivery enabled |
| **Iterations** | 10,000 total messages measured across 1,000 iterations of 100-message batches |
| **Metric** | Messages per second (msgs/sec) |
| **Warm-up** | 3 warmup iterations (each sending the configured message batch) are discarded before measurement begins |

Each binary is started in daemon mode listening on a designated localhost port. The `swaks` tool delivers 10,000 individual SMTP transactions (one message per TCP connection) to the daemon. Total wall-clock time is recorded and throughput is computed as total messages divided by elapsed seconds. Multiple runs are aggregated to produce mean, median, standard deviation, and percentile statistics.

### 2.2 Fork-per-Connection Latency

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Tool** | `hyperfine` __SYS_HYPERFINE_VERSION__ |
| **Connections** | 1,000 sequential SMTP connections per iteration |
| **Measurement** | Time-to-first-response (220 banner) after TCP connect |
| **Iterations** | 100 iterations per binary |
| **Metric** | Mean latency in milliseconds (ms) |
| **Warm-up** | 10 warm-up runs excluded from statistics |

Each binary is started in daemon mode. A custom connection script opens 1,000 sequential TCP connections to the SMTP port and measures the time from the completion of the TCP handshake to receipt of the SMTP 220 greeting banner. This measures the fork-per-connection overhead inherent in the Exim architecture. The `hyperfine` tool coordinates repeated runs and computes statistical summaries.

### 2.3 Peak RSS Memory

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Tool** | `/usr/bin/time -v` (GNU time verbose mode) |
| **Message Size** | 10 MB (generated test message) |
| **Iterations** | 100 iterations per binary |
| **Metric** | Maximum Resident Set Size in kilobytes (KB) |
| **Delivery Mode** | Single-message injection via command-line (`exim -bs`) |

A 10 MB test message is injected into each binary using the batch SMTP mode (`exim -bs`). GNU time captures the maximum resident set size (RSS) of the child process during message processing. The test is repeated 100 times and the mean, median, and maximum RSS values are recorded. This measures the memory overhead of the runtime, allocator, and message-processing pipeline.

### 2.4 Config Parse Time

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Tool** | `hyperfine` __SYS_HYPERFINE_VERSION__ |
| **Config File** | `src/src/configure.default` |
| **Mode** | Config print mode (`exim -bP`) |
| **Iterations** | 1,000 iterations per binary |
| **Metric** | Mean parse time in milliseconds (ms) |
| **Warm-up** | 50 warm-up runs excluded from statistics |

Each binary is invoked with the `-bP` flag, which parses the full configuration file and prints the parsed configuration to stdout. The `hyperfine` tool measures wall-clock time for each invocation across 1,000 iterations. This benchmark is directional (informational) and does not have a hard pass/fail threshold, though it provides valuable insight into the relative efficiency of the configuration parser implementation.

---

## Results

### Summary

| Metric | C Exim | Rust Exim | Delta (%) | Threshold | Verdict |
|--------|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| SMTP Throughput (msgs/sec) | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_C__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_RUST__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_DELTA__% | ≤ 10% slower | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_VERDICT__ |
| Fork Latency (ms) | __FORK_LATENCY_C__ | __FORK_LATENCY_RUST__ | __FORK_LATENCY_DELTA__% | ≤ 5% slower | __FORK_LATENCY_VERDICT__ |
| Peak RSS (KB) | __PEAK_RSS_C__ | __PEAK_RSS_RUST__ | __PEAK_RSS_DELTA__% | ≤ 120% of C | __PEAK_RSS_VERDICT__ |
| Config Parse (ms) | __CONFIG_PARSE_C__ | __CONFIG_PARSE_RUST__ | __CONFIG_PARSE_DELTA__% | Directional | __CONFIG_PARSE_VERDICT__ |

> **Note:** A negative delta percentage for throughput indicates the Rust binary is faster.
> A positive delta percentage for latency, memory, or parse time indicates the Rust binary uses more resources.

---

### SMTP Transaction Throughput

**Acceptance Threshold:** Rust Exim must achieve throughput within 10% of C Exim (i.e., no more than 10% slower).

#### Test Parameters

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Message Count | 10,000 |
| Delivery Method | Localhost loopback |
| Configuration | `src/src/configure.default` |
| SMTP Port (C) | __SMTP_PORT_C__ |
| SMTP Port (Rust) | __SMTP_PORT_RUST__ |

#### Detailed Results

| Statistic | C Exim (msgs/sec) | Rust Exim (msgs/sec) |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Mean** | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_C_MEAN__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_RUST_MEAN__ |
| **Median** | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_C_MEDIAN__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_RUST_MEDIAN__ |
| **Std Dev** | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_C_STDDEV__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_RUST_STDDEV__ |
| **P95** | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_C_P95__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_RUST_P95__ |
| **P99** | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_C_P99__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_RUST_P99__ |
| **Min** | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_C_MIN__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_RUST_MIN__ |
| **Max** | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_C_MAX__ | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_RUST_MAX__ |

#### Verdict

**Delta:** __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_DELTA__% · **Result:** __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_VERDICT__

__SMTP_THROUGHPUT_OBSERVATION__

### Fork-per-Connection Latency

**Acceptance Threshold:** Rust Exim fork-per-connection latency must be within 5% of C Exim (i.e., no more than 5% slower).

#### Test Parameters

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Sequential Connections | 1,000 |
| Measurement | Time-to-first-response (220 banner) |
| Iterations | 100 |

**Assessment Type:** Directional comparison (informational — no hard pass/fail threshold).

#### Test Parameters

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|

|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Mean** | __CONFIG_PARSE_C_MEAN__ | __CONFIG_PARSE_RUST_MEAN__ |
| **Median** | __CONFIG_PARSE_C_MEDIAN__ | __CONFIG_PARSE_RUST_MEDIAN__ |
| **Std Dev** | __CONFIG_PARSE_C_STDDEV__ | __CONFIG_PARSE_RUST_STDDEV__ |
| **Min** | __CONFIG_PARSE_C_MIN__ | __CONFIG_PARSE_RUST_MIN__ |
| **Max** | __CONFIG_PARSE_C_MAX__ | __CONFIG_PARSE_RUST_MAX__ |

#### Verdict

**Direction:** __CONFIG_PARSE_DIRECTION__ · **Result:** __CONFIG_PARSE_VERDICT__

__CONFIG_PARSE_OBSERVATION__

> **Note:** Config parse time is reported for directional insight only. The Exim configuration parser is invoked once at startup; parse-time differences have negligible impact on steady-state throughput.

## Overall Assessment

### Gate Summary

| Gate | Metric | Threshold | Result | Verdict |
|------|--------|-----------|--------|---------|
| Gate 3a | SMTP Throughput | Rust within 10% of C | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_DELTA__% delta | __SMTP_THROUGHPUT_VERDICT__ |
| Gate 3b | Fork Latency | Rust within 5% of C | __FORK_LATENCY_DELTA__% delta | __FORK_LATENCY_VERDICT__ |
| Gate 3c | Peak RSS Memory | Rust ≤ 120% of C | __PEAK_RSS_DELTA__% delta | __PEAK_RSS_VERDICT__ |
| Gate 3d | Config Parse Time | Directional comparison | __CONFIG_PARSE_DELTA__% delta | __CONFIG_PARSE_VERDICT__ |

## Appendix

### A.1 Raw Data

Benchmark results are saved in structured formats alongside this report for further analysis:

| File | Format | Contents |
|------|--------|----------|
| `bench/results/smtp_throughput.json` | JSON | Per-iteration throughput measurements |
| `bench/results/fork_latency.json` | JSON | Per-iteration latency measurements (hyperfine output) |
| `bench/results/peak_rss.csv` | CSV | Per-iteration RSS measurements |
| `bench/results/config_parse.json` | JSON | Per-iteration parse time measurements (hyperfine output) |
| `bench/results/system_info.json` | JSON | System specification captured at benchmark time |

### A.2 Reproducing the Benchmarks

To reproduce these benchmarks on your system:

1. **Build the C binary:**
   ```
   cd src && make
   ```

2. **Build the Rust binary:**
   ```
   cargo build --release
   ```

3. **Run the benchmark suite:**
   ```
   bash bench/run_benchmarks.sh
   ```

   The script will:
   - Detect both binaries automatically
   - Capture system specification
   - Execute all four benchmark tests
   - Generate this report with populated results
   - Save raw data to `bench/results/`

4. **Optional: Run individual benchmarks:**
   ```
   bash bench/run_benchmarks.sh --test smtp_throughput
   bash bench/run_benchmarks.sh --test fork_latency
   bash bench/run_benchmarks.sh --test peak_rss
   bash bench/run_benchmarks.sh --test config_parse
   ```

### A.3 Placeholder Reference

This report template uses `__PLACEHOLDER_NAME__` markers that are programmatically replaced by `bench/run_benchmarks.sh` using `sed`. The following placeholders are defined:

| Placeholder | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `__REPORT_DATE__` | Timestamp when the report was generated |
| `__SYS_*__` | System specification values (CPU, RAM, OS, kernel, compiler versions) |
| `__EXIM_*__` | Exim binary paths and build configuration |
| `__SMTP_THROUGHPUT_*__` | SMTP throughput test results (C, Rust, delta, statistics, verdict) |
| `__FORK_LATENCY_*__` | Fork latency test results (C, Rust, delta, statistics, verdict) |
| `__PEAK_RSS_*__` | Peak RSS memory test results (C, Rust, delta, statistics, verdict) |
| `__CONFIG_PARSE_*__` | Config parse time test results (C, Rust, delta, statistics, verdict) |
| `__OVERALL_VERDICT__` | Overall migration readiness assessment |
| `__THRESHOLD_WARNINGS__` | Warnings for metrics approaching threshold limits |
| `__MIGRATION_READINESS_ASSESSMENT__` | Narrative assessment of migration readiness |

### A.4 Acceptance Criteria Reference

These thresholds are defined in the Exim C → Rust Migration Agent Action Plan (AAP §0.7.5):

| Metric | Threshold | Rationale |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| SMTP Throughput | Rust within 10% of C | Core operational metric for production mail handling |
| Fork Latency | Rust within 5% of C | Critical for high-concurrency SMTP acceptance |
| Peak RSS Memory | Rust ≤ 120% of C | Memory budget for constrained environments |
| Config Parse Time | Directional (no hard limit) | One-time startup cost with minimal operational impact |

### A.5 Tools and Versions

| Tool | Version | Purpose |
|------|---------|---------|
| `hyperfine` | __SYS_HYPERFINE_VERSION__ | Binary-level benchmark timing with statistical analysis |
| `swaks` | latest | SMTP transaction generation for throughput testing |
| `/usr/bin/time` | GNU time | Process resource usage measurement (RSS) |
| `sed` | GNU sed | Template placeholder substitution in report generation |
| `jq` | latest | JSON processing for result aggregation |
